_meta.source: Primary sources: commonapp.org, studentaid.gov, cssprofile.collegeboard.org, satsuite.collegeboard.org, act.org, apstudents.collegeboard.org, admission.universityofcalifornia.edu, travel.state.gov. Grade 9-11 practice rows synthesize BigFuture (bigfuture.collegeboard.org) checklist conventions.
_meta.notes: Rules, not constants: fixed_annual rows survive year rollover; pattern rows must be phrased as 'typically' and cross-checked against data/test_dates.json; college_specific rows resolve from the workspace colleges.json. Year math: grade g month m falls in calendar year grad_year-13+g when m>=8, else grad_year-12+g.

date_type_legend.fixed_annual: same calendar date every year — state plainly
date_type_legend.pattern: recurring convention — phrase as 'typically', link verify URL
date_type_legend.college_specific: resolve from colleges.json; always add verify caveat
date_type_legend.relative: offset from another event or student action

milestones.title: Settle into the hardest appropriate course load; join 2-4 activities
milestones.action: Freshman year is about habits, not colleges: strong grades in the most rigorous appropriate courses, and exploring a few activities with an eye toward depth later. Nothing this year is fatal.

milestones.title: Plan the 4-year course rigor arc with the counselor; lock a meaningful summer
milestones.action: Map honors → AP/IB progression for grades 10-12 during course selection. Plan a summer with substance: a program, a job, or a self-driven project.

milestones.title: PSAT 10 / practice PSAT sitting (register through school)
milestones.action: A no-stakes diagnostic. Registration happens through the school, not College Board.

milestones.title: Take one official SAT practice test and one ACT practice test; compare
milestones.action: Compare percentiles (not raw comfort) and commit to one test. Free official SAT prep lives on Khan Academy. Also: informal campus visits when travel allows, and plan the summer.

milestones.title: PSAT/NMSQT — the National Merit qualifier
milestones.action: The junior-year sitting is the one that counts for National Merit. Register through school in September.

milestones.title: Begin SAT/ACT prep; build the initial college list
milestones.action: 8-12 weeks of steady prep before the first spring sitting beats cramming. Start a first reach/match/safety list (/admit:college-list) — it will change, that's fine.

milestones.title: Common App essay prompts announced (typically late February)
milestones.action: Prompts for the coming cycle are announced — safe to start thinking about material, no need to draft yet.

milestones.title: First official SAT or ACT sitting

date_rule.description: SAT: typically March/May/June Saturdays; ACT: February/April/June

milestones.action: Sit the chosen test in spring of junior year, leaving room for one retake in early fall. Registration closes roughly 2 weeks ahead (SAT) / 5 weeks ahead (ACT).

milestones.title: AP exams (first two full weeks of May)
milestones.action: AP exams run the first two full weeks of May. Scores arrive early July.

milestones.title: Ask 2 junior-year teachers for recommendations before summer
milestones.action: Ask in person, in spring, while you're fresh in the teacher's mind — then provide a brag sheet. Not needed for a UC-only list.

milestones.title: Counselor meeting: transcript check, rigor narrative, senior schedule
milestones.action: Verify the transcript, confirm senior-year rigor, and discuss the college list. Ask how your school handles transcript and school-report requests.

milestones.title: Run 3 Net Price Calculators and have the budget conversation
milestones.action: Before the list is final: run the Net Price Calculator at three candidate colleges and agree on a real budget. This conversation in junior spring prevents heartbreak in April of senior year.

milestones.title: Draft the personal essay; finalize the college list
milestones.action: The single highest-leverage summer task: a real draft of the personal essay (prompts have been out since February) and a settled list. AP scores arrive early July; plan any fall retake now.

milestones.title: Common App opens / refreshes for the new cycle
milestones.action: Create or roll over the account (the Common App tab and personal essay survive the annual refresh). Enter activities, add colleges, check each college's supplement list.

milestones.title: UC application opens (filing window comes later)
milestones.action: Start the UC application: activities, PIQ planning. You can work in it from Aug 1 even though filing opens Oct 1.

milestones.title: Request transcripts; confirm recommenders with brag sheets
milestones.action: Give recommenders your resume/brag sheet and your deadline list. Request transcripts per your school's process. Two weeks of buffer beats an October scramble.

milestones.title: FAFSA opens
milestones.action: File within the first weeks — state and college priority deadlines can be as early as December. Create FSA IDs (student + one parent contributor) a week before you file.
milestones.verify_note: State priority deadlines vary — always check the studentaid.gov deadline page for your state.

milestones.title: CSS Profile opens (colleges that use it for their own aid)
milestones.action: Check which of your colleges require the CSS Profile and their priority dates. Fee waivers apply below the income threshold — verify current terms on the Profile site.

milestones.title: UC filing window opens (Oct 1 - Nov 30, firm)
milestones.action: The UC window is Oct 1 - Nov 30 and lands a full month before most RD deadlines — Californians' fall calendar is front-loaded. Don't plan on the occasional few-day extension.

milestones.title: October SAT/ACT — last comfortable sitting for Nov 1 deadlines

date_rule.description: typically the first Saturday of October (SAT)

milestones.action: Scores from October sittings generally reach ED/EA colleges in time; later is a gamble. Registration closes ~2 weeks ahead (SAT) / ~5 weeks (ACT).

milestones.title: ED / EA / REA applications due (most colleges Nov 1)
milestones.action: Most early deadlines are Nov 1; some are Nov 15 or Dec 1. ED is binding — the family financial conversation must already have happened. Verify every date on the college's page.

milestones.title: UC filing deadline (Nov 30, firm)
milestones.action: All four PIQs and the full application must be submitted. Occasional extensions happen; never plan on one.

milestones.title: CSU priority filing closes (typically early December)

date_rule.description: typically early December — varies by campus and program

milestones.action: Cal State Apply priority window typically runs Oct 1 to early December. Impacted campuses/programs close hard — verify per campus.

milestones.title: December SAT/ACT — last sitting most RD colleges accept

date_rule.description: typically the first Saturday of December (SAT)

milestones.action: The December sitting is the practical end of the testing road for January deadlines.

milestones.title: ED decisions arrive (typically mid-December)
milestones.action: Whatever the ED result, keep finishing RD applications over winter break — deferral and denial both need a live RD slate. Accepted ED = withdraw other applications.

milestones.title: RD and ED2 applications due (cluster Jan 1-15)
milestones.action: RD deadlines cluster Jan 1-15. Mid-year reports go out via the counselor. State FAFSA priority deadlines begin landing — check your state.

milestones.title: IDOC / verification uploads if requested (deadlines are midnight ET)

date_rule.description: only after College Board emails an IDOC request post-CSS-submission

milestones.action: If a college uses IDOC, College Board emails you after the CSS Profile is submitted. Upload tax documents promptly; IDOC deadlines are midnight Eastern.

milestones.title: RD decisions arrive (mid-March to early April)

date_rule.description: typically mid-March through early April; Ivies release together in late March

milestones.action: As decisions and aid offers arrive: drop award letters into aid/award-letters/ and compare offers line by line (/admit:aid) — 4-year cost, not year-1 sticker.

milestones.title: National reply date — deposit at exactly one college
milestones.action: Deposit at one college by May 1 (double-depositing violates policy). Waitlist movement runs May-July — deposit somewhere regardless.

milestones.title: AP exams (first two full weeks of May)
milestones.action: Offers are conditional on finishing strong. Send AP scores officially to the enrolling college in July.

milestones.title: Final transcript to the enrolling college
milestones.action: Ask the counselor to send the final transcript after graduation; the enrolling college requires it.

milestones.title: English proficiency test (TOEFL/IELTS/Duolingo) — test by Sep-Oct for Nov deadlines

date_rule.description: test by Sep-Oct for Nov 1 deadlines; by Oct-Nov for January deadlines — colleges need delivery time

milestones.action: TOEFL scores post in roughly 4-8 days; IELTS and Duolingo vary — build in delivery time. Check each college's minimum scores and waiver rules (e.g., English-medium schooling).

milestones.title: Credential evaluation for non-US transcripts (order 4-8 weeks before first deadline)

date_rule.description: order 4-8 weeks before the earliest application deadline, if a college requires it

milestones.action: Some colleges require a course-by-course evaluation (WES, ECE) for non-US transcripts; many evaluate in-house. Check each college first — evaluations take weeks and cost money.

milestones.title: Prepare financial documentation (certification of finances, bank statements)

date_rule.description: at application or at admission, per college — the same documents later support the I-20

milestones.action: Most US colleges are need-aware for international applicants. Prepare proof of funding early; it's required for the I-20 after enrollment anyway.

milestones.title: I-20 → SEVIS fee → DS-160 → visa interview (start immediately after depositing)
milestones.action: Request the I-20 from the college right after depositing → pay the SEVIS I-901 fee → file DS-160 → book the embassy interview early (wait times vary widely by post). F-1 can be issued up to 365 days before the program start; US entry is allowed within 30 days of the start date.
